parts.context: A conversation between a customer and a travel agent at a travel agency.
parts.audio_script:
Agent: Good morning, Wanderlust Travel, this is Sarah speaking. How can I help you today?
Customer: Oh, hi. Um, I was hoping to book a train ticket to Manchester, please.
Agent: Certainly. May I have your name, please?
Customer: Yes, it’s Michael Turner.
Agent: Thank you, Mr. Turner. And when would you like to travel?
Customer: Uh, let me think... I’d prefer to go on Tuesday, if possible. Is that available?
Agent: Let me check... Yes, there are several trains on Tuesday. Do you have a preferred time?
Customer: I’d like to leave in the morning, maybe around 9 or 10?
Agent: There’s a train at 9:15 and another at 10:30. Which would you prefer?
Customer: The 9:15 sounds good.
Agent: Great. Would you like a return ticket or just one way?
Customer: Oh, just one way, please. I’m not sure when I’ll be coming back yet.
Agent: No problem. And will you be travelling alone?
Customer: Yes, just me.
Agent: Okay. Do you have any railcards or discounts?
Customer: Um, no, I don’t think so.
Agent: That’s fine. The standard fare for a single adult ticket on the 9:15 train to Manchester is £42. Would you like to reserve a seat?
Customer: Yes, please. I’d prefer a window seat if possible.
Agent: Let me see... Yes, I can reserve a window seat for you. Would you like to pay now or collect and pay at the station?
Customer: I’ll pay now, please. Can I use my credit card?
Agent: Of course. I’ll just need your card details. Also, would you like your ticket emailed to you or printed for collection?
Customer: Emailed, please. My email is michael.turner@gmail.com.
Agent: Thank you. I’ll just confirm your booking: One adult, single ticket to Manchester on Tuesday, departing at 9:15, window seat reserved, ticket to be emailed. Is that all correct?
Customer: Yes, that’s right.
Agent: Perfect. I’ll process your payment now... Okay, that’s gone through. You should receive your ticket in your inbox within the next few minutes. Is there anything else I can help you with?
Customer: No, that’s everything. Thanks very much for your help.
Agent: You’re welcome, Mr. Turner. Have a pleasant journey!
Customer: Thank you. Goodbye.
Agent: Goodbye.

question_sets.instruction: Complete the notes below. Write ONE WORD ONLY for each answer.

questions.question_text: Customer wants to travel on ______.
Preferred departure time is in the ______.
Customer chooses the ______ train.
Type of ticket requested: ______.

Ticket will be sent by ______.

questions.question_text: How does the customer pay for the ticket?

Who is the ticket for?
options: A. The customer and a friend

parts.context: A monologue by a museum guide about the new City History Museum.
parts.audio_script:
Good afternoon, everyone, and welcome to the City History Museum. My name’s Helen, and I’ll be giving you a brief introduction before you begin your visit. Now, before we start, I’d just like to remind you that the museum is open from 10 a.m. to 5 p.m. every day except Mondays, when we’re closed for maintenance. If you need a map, you can pick one up at the entrance desk, and lockers for your bags are available just to the left of the main doors.

The museum is divided into three main sections. On the ground floor, you’ll find the Ancient City exhibit, which covers the earliest settlements in this area, dating back over two thousand years. There are some fascinating artefacts, including pottery, tools, and even a reconstructed house from the Roman period. If you’re interested in archaeology, I’d recommend spending some time there.

Moving upstairs, the first floor is dedicated to the Industrial Era. This part of the museum is very interactive, with working models of old machinery and hands-on displays. Children especially enjoy the steam engine demonstration, which takes place every hour on the hour. Oh, and don’t miss the textile workshop, where you can try weaving on a traditional loom. It’s a bit tricky at first, but our staff are always happy to help.

On the top floor, we have the Modern City gallery. Here, you can learn about the city’s development over the last hundred years, including its role in the two world wars and the growth of its famous music scene. There’s a special exhibition at the moment on local artists, which is well worth a look.

If you get hungry during your visit, the café is located on the ground floor, just past the Ancient City exhibit. It serves a range of hot and cold drinks, sandwiches, and cakes. There’s also a gift shop near the exit, where you can buy souvenirs, books, and postcards.

Finally, a quick note about accessibility: all floors are accessible by lift, and there are accessible toilets on every level. If you need any assistance, please don’t hesitate to ask a member of staff. We hope you enjoy your visit and discover something new about our city’s rich history. Thank you.

question_sets.instruction: Match each museum section with the correct description. Write the correct letter, A–E, next to questions 11–15.

questions.question_text: Ancient City
Industrial Era
Modern City gallery
Textile workshop
Café

question_sets.instruction: Complete the sentences below. Write NO MORE THAN TWO WORDS for each answer.

questions.question_text: The museum is closed on ______.
The steam engine demonstration happens every ______.
The textile workshop allows visitors to try ______.
The special exhibition is about ______ artists.
Accessible toilets are available on ______ level.

parts.context: A conversation between two university students, Anna and Ben, and their tutor, Dr. Lee, about a group project.
parts.audio_script:
Dr. Lee: Good afternoon, Anna, Ben. Thanks for coming in to discuss your group project. How’s it going so far?
Anna: Um, it’s going okay, I think. We’ve divided up the research, but we’re a bit unsure about the structure.
Ben: Yeah, we’ve found lots of information, but we’re not sure what to include in each section.
Dr. Lee: That’s quite common at this stage. Have you looked at the marking criteria?
Anna: Yes, but, uh, we’re still a bit confused about the difference between analysis and description.
Dr. Lee: Right, so, description is just stating the facts, but analysis means you need to explain why those facts are important. For example, don’t just say what happened, but discuss the reasons and the consequences.
Ben: Okay, that makes sense. Should we include case studies?
Dr. Lee: Absolutely. Case studies are a great way to show you understand the theory. Just make sure you link them back to your main argument.
Anna: We were also wondering about the presentation. Do we have to use PowerPoint?
Dr. Lee: It’s not compulsory, but it does help to organise your ideas. If you prefer, you can use posters or handouts, but PowerPoint is usually the easiest.
Ben: And, um, how long should the presentation be?
Dr. Lee: Ten minutes, plus five minutes for questions. Try to keep it clear and concise.
Anna: We’re a bit worried about timing. We tend to talk too much!
Dr. Lee: Practise together a few times, and maybe use a timer. That usually helps.
Ben: One more thing—do we need to hand in a written report as well?
Dr. Lee: Yes, you do. The report should be about 2,000 words, and it’s due a week after your presentation.
Anna: Okay, thanks. And, um, if we have problems, can we email you?
Dr. Lee: Of course. I’m happy to answer questions by email, or you can come to my office hours on Wednesdays.
Ben: Great, thank you, Dr. Lee.
Dr. Lee: No problem. Good luck with your project, and let me know if you need any more help.
Anna: Thanks!
Ben: Thanks, bye.

question_sets.instruction: Choose the correct letter, A, B or C.

questions.question_text: What are Anna and Ben struggling with?
What does Dr. Lee say about analysis?
What does Dr. Lee recommend for the presentation?
How long should the presentation be?
When is the written report due?
options: A. The same day as the presentation
B. A week after the presentation
C. Two weeks after the presentation

question_sets.instruction: Complete the sentences below. Write NO MORE THAN TWO WORDS for each answer.

questions.question_text: Anna and Ben have already divided up the ______.

parts.context: A lecture by Professor Carter on the topic of urban green spaces.
parts.audio_script:
Good morning, everyone. Today, I’d like to talk about urban green spaces and their importance in modern cities. Now, when we think of green spaces, we often imagine parks, gardens, and even small patches of grass by the roadside. But, uh, the definition is actually much broader. It includes everything from large public parks to community gardens, green roofs, and even tree-lined streets.

So, why are these spaces so important? Well, firstly, they provide a place for people to relax and exercise. Studies have shown that access to green spaces can improve both physical and mental health. For example, people who live near parks tend to be more active and report lower levels of stress. There’s also evidence that children who play in green areas develop better social skills.

Another key benefit is environmental. Green spaces help to reduce air pollution by absorbing carbon dioxide and other pollutants. They also help to cool cities, which is especially important as global temperatures rise. Trees and plants provide shade and release moisture into the air, making urban areas more comfortable during heatwaves.

Now, let’s talk about biodiversity. Urban green spaces can support a surprising variety of wildlife, from birds and insects to small mammals. Even a small garden can become a habitat for bees and butterflies, which are essential for pollination. In fact, some cities have introduced wildflower meadows in parks to encourage more species.

Of course, there are challenges. As cities grow, there’s often pressure to build on green land. This can lead to a loss of habitats and fewer places for people to enjoy nature. It’s important for city planners to balance development with the need to protect and create green spaces. Some cities have introduced policies to ensure that new buildings include green roofs or vertical gardens.

Finally, I’d like to mention community involvement. Many successful green spaces are managed by local volunteers who organise events, plant trees, and keep the area clean. This not only improves the environment but also brings people together and strengthens community ties.

In conclusion, urban green spaces are vital for healthy, sustainable cities. They offer social, environmental, and economic benefits, and it’s up to all of us to protect and enhance them. Thank you for listening.

question_sets.instruction: Choose the correct letter, A, B or C.

options: B. Parks, gardens, and green roofs

How do green spaces help the environment?

C. Lack of interest from city planners

passages.title: The Evolution of Urban Green Spaces
passages.content:
Urban green spaces, such as parks, gardens, and tree-lined avenues, have become integral components of modern cities. Their evolution reflects changing societal values, urban planning philosophies, and environmental awareness. In the 19th century, the rapid industrialization of cities led to overcrowding, pollution, and a decline in public health. In response, urban planners and reformers advocated for the creation of public parks as a means to provide city dwellers with access to nature, fresh air, and recreational opportunities. The establishment of Central Park in New York City in 1857, designed by Frederick Law Olmsted and Calvert Vaux, marked a turning point in the conceptualization of urban green spaces. The park was envisioned not merely as a recreational facility but as a social equalizer, offering respite to people of all backgrounds.

Throughout the 20th century, the role of green spaces expanded. The Garden City movement, initiated by Ebenezer Howard in the United Kingdom, proposed the integration of green belts and communal gardens within urban developments. This approach aimed to balance the benefits of urban living with the health advantages of rural environments. In many European cities, green spaces were systematically incorporated into urban plans, resulting in the proliferation of public parks, botanical gardens, and tree-lined boulevards.

In recent decades, the significance of urban green spaces has been further underscored by growing concerns over climate change, biodiversity loss, and urban heat islands. Green spaces are now recognized for their ecological functions, including carbon sequestration, stormwater management, and habitat provision for urban wildlife. Studies have demonstrated that access to green spaces can improve mental health, reduce stress, and foster social cohesion among urban residents. Consequently, city governments worldwide have launched initiatives to expand and enhance green infrastructure, often involving community participation in the design and maintenance of local parks.

Despite these advances, challenges persist. Urban land is often scarce and expensive, leading to competition between green space preservation and other forms of development. In some cities, green spaces are unevenly distributed, with affluent neighborhoods enjoying greater access than marginalized communities. This disparity has prompted calls for more equitable planning and investment in green infrastructure. Additionally, the maintenance of urban green spaces requires ongoing funding and management, which can be difficult to sustain in the face of budget constraints.

Innovative solutions are emerging to address these challenges. Vertical gardens, green roofs, and pocket parks are being implemented in densely built environments where traditional parks are not feasible. Urban agriculture initiatives, such as community gardens and rooftop farms, are also gaining popularity, providing both environmental and social benefits. Furthermore, digital technologies are being harnessed to monitor the health of urban vegetation and engage citizens in stewardship activities.

The evolution of urban green spaces illustrates the dynamic interplay between environmental, social, and economic factors in city planning. As urban populations continue to grow, the importance of accessible, well-maintained green spaces is likely to increase. Their continued development will depend on innovative design, inclusive policies, and sustained public engagement.

question_sets.instruction: Do the following statements agree with the information given in the passage? Write TRUE if the statement agrees with the information, FALSE if the statement contradicts the information, or NOT GIVEN if there is no information on this.

Green spaces can help reduce the effects of urban heat islands.
All cities have an equal distribution of green spaces.

questions.question_text: Describes the historical context that led to the creation of urban parks.

Highlights the role of the Garden City movement.

passages.title: The Impact of Artificial Intelligence on Scientific Research
passages.content:
Artificial Intelligence (AI) has rapidly transformed the landscape of scientific research, introducing new methodologies and accelerating the pace of discovery. The integration of AI into research processes has enabled scientists to analyze vast datasets, identify patterns, and generate hypotheses with unprecedented speed and accuracy. This technological revolution is not without controversy, as it raises questions about the reliability of AI-generated results, the role of human intuition, and the ethical implications of automated decision-making.

One of the most significant contributions of AI to science is its capacity for data analysis. In fields such as genomics, climate science, and particle physics, researchers are confronted with enormous volumes of data that would be impossible to process manually. Machine learning algorithms can sift through these datasets, uncovering correlations and anomalies that might otherwise go unnoticed. For example, in drug discovery, AI systems have been used to predict the efficacy of chemical compounds, dramatically reducing the time required to identify promising candidates for clinical trials.

However, the reliance on AI also introduces new challenges. Machine learning models are only as good as the data on which they are trained. If the input data is biased or incomplete, the resulting analyses may be flawed. Moreover, the 'black box' nature of many AI systems makes it difficult for researchers to understand how specific conclusions are reached. This lack of transparency can undermine trust in AI-driven research, particularly in fields where reproducibility and interpretability are paramount.

The debate over the role of human intuition in scientific discovery has intensified with the rise of AI. While machines excel at processing information and recognizing patterns, they lack the creativity and contextual understanding that characterize human thought. Some argue that AI should be viewed as a tool to augment, rather than replace, human researchers. Collaborative approaches, in which AI systems generate hypotheses that are then tested and refined by scientists, are increasingly common. This synergy has the potential to accelerate innovation while preserving the critical role of human judgment.

Ethical considerations are also at the forefront of discussions about AI in science. Automated systems can inadvertently perpetuate existing biases or make decisions that have far-reaching consequences. For instance, in medical research, AI-driven diagnostic tools must be rigorously validated to ensure they do not disadvantage certain patient groups. The development of ethical guidelines and regulatory frameworks is essential to ensure that AI is used responsibly and equitably in scientific contexts.

Looking ahead, the influence of AI on scientific research is likely to grow. Advances in natural language processing, computer vision, and robotics are expanding the range of tasks that AI can perform. At the same time, interdisciplinary collaboration between computer scientists, domain experts, and ethicists will be crucial to harness the benefits of AI while mitigating its risks. Ultimately, the successful integration of AI into scientific research will depend on a careful balance between technological innovation, human oversight, and ethical responsibility.

question_sets.instruction: Choose the correct letter, A, B, C, or D.

passages.title: The Paradox of Choice in Modern Consumer Culture
passages.content:
In contemporary society, the proliferation of consumer goods and services has led to an unprecedented expansion of choice. Supermarkets, online retailers, and service providers offer a dizzying array of options, from dozens of breakfast cereals to hundreds of smartphone models. While the abundance of choice is often celebrated as a hallmark of economic progress and individual freedom, a growing body of research suggests that excessive choice can have counterintuitive and even detrimental effects on consumer well-being.

The concept of the 'paradox of choice' was popularized by psychologist Barry Schwartz, who argued that while some choice is undoubtedly beneficial, too much choice can lead to anxiety, indecision, and dissatisfaction. Schwartz's research demonstrated that when individuals are confronted with a large number of options, they may experience 'choice overload,' a cognitive state characterized by difficulty in making decisions and a heightened fear of making the wrong choice. This phenomenon is not limited to trivial purchases; it extends to significant life decisions such as selecting a career path or choosing a healthcare plan.

One explanation for the paradox of choice lies in the psychological mechanisms underlying decision-making. When faced with many alternatives, consumers are compelled to engage in extensive information processing, comparing features, prices, and potential outcomes. This process can be mentally exhausting and may lead to 'analysis paralysis,' where the sheer volume of information inhibits action. Moreover, the presence of numerous options increases the likelihood of post-decision regret, as individuals imagine that an unchosen alternative might have yielded greater satisfaction.

Paradoxically, the expectation that more choice leads to greater happiness is often contradicted by empirical evidence. Studies have shown that consumers presented with fewer options tend to make decisions more quickly and report higher levels of satisfaction with their choices. In contrast, those exposed to extensive choice sets are more likely to second-guess their decisions and feel less content, even when the objective quality of their selection is high. This suggests that the psychological costs of excessive choice may outweigh its potential benefits.

The implications of the paradox of choice extend beyond individual consumers to broader societal and economic domains. Marketers and policymakers must grapple with the challenge of designing choice environments that maximize welfare without overwhelming individuals. Some companies have responded by curating product selections or offering personalized recommendations to simplify decision-making. In the public sector, 'choice architecture'—the deliberate structuring of options—has been employed to guide citizens toward beneficial behaviors, such as enrolling in retirement savings plans or selecting healthy foods.

Critics of the paradox of choice thesis argue that the negative effects of abundant choice are overstated and that individuals can adapt to complex choice environments through learning and experience. They contend that the benefits of choice, including autonomy and self-expression, should not be underestimated. Furthermore, cultural factors may mediate the impact of choice overload, with some societies placing greater value on individual decision-making than others.

Ultimately, the paradox of choice highlights the complex interplay between freedom, satisfaction, and psychological well-being in modern consumer culture. While the availability of options is a defining feature of contemporary life, it is clear that more is not always better. Striking the right balance between abundance and simplicity remains a central challenge for individuals, businesses, and policymakers alike.

question_sets.instruction: Complete the summary below. Choose NO MORE THAN TWO WORDS from the passage for each answer.

questions.question_text: The 'paradox of choice' refers to the idea that having too many options can result in negative outcomes such as anxiety and ________.

The process of comparing many options can lead to mental exhaustion and ________ paralysis.
Empirical studies indicate that people with fewer choices often feel more ________ with their decisions.
Some companies use ________ recommendations to help consumers make decisions.
The deliberate structuring of options to influence behavior is known as choice ________.

tasks.instruction: You should spend about 20 minutes on this task. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
tasks.data_description: The table below shows the percentage of households in five different countries (Canada, Germany, Japan, Brazil, and South Africa) that owned various types of electronic devices in the years 2000 and 2020. The devices listed are televisions, computers, and smartphones. In 2000, television ownership was highest in Canada at 95%, followed by Germany at 92%, Japan at 90%, Brazil at 75%, and South Africa at 60%. By 2020, television ownership increased slightly in all countries, with Canada and Germany both at 98%, Japan at 97%, Brazil at 85%, and South Africa at 70%. Computer ownership in 2000 was 60% in Canada, 55% in Germany, 50% in Japan, 20% in Brazil, and 10% in South Africa. In 2020, these figures rose to 90% in Canada, 88% in Germany, 85% in Japan, 60% in Brazil, and 40% in South Africa. Smartphone ownership was negligible in all countries in 2000 (all below 5%), but by 2020, it had risen dramatically: 95% in Canada, 93% in Germany, 90% in Japan, 80% in Brazil, and 65% in South Africa.

tasks.instruction: You should spend about 40 minutes on this task. Write about the following topic. Give reasons for your answer and include any relevant examples from your own knowledge or experience.
tasks.prompt: Some people believe that the primary purpose of education is to prepare individuals to be useful members of society. Others think that education should help people achieve personal ambitions. Discuss both these views and give your own opinion.
